item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Items expelled were included but not limited to…"

I think this would work better as "Items expelled were…", "Items expelled included but were not limited to…", or "Some items expelled included, but were not limited to…"

Wow, that delved into some kind of inanity. I think it was fun?

..Yeah, it was fun. +1. I, for one, welcome our inevitable future destruction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this as an experiment. I feel conflicted about it as an article. Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't mind intentionally surreal writing, but the disjointedness and awkward phrasing made me lose any interest in what was happening. I couldn't care.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not an entry? Well, please ignore the parts that mention the contest then.

Regardless, my point still stands. When I say strife or conflict in the article, I don't mean that the there is a sense of competition with people trying to give some twist with what was given, I mean that the general motif and ideas presented in the article comes off as very unfocused. There's a certain level of disagreement within the article's main opus.

You may be right in the idea that there is some level of sensible logic here, but it's buried under a lot of filler that it could probably make more sense if it was more streamlined and focused more on the story that it might emerge stronger than its current form.

That's just my two cents. I like the imagery along with the creativity behind it all, but I personally can't properly grasp the main narrative chunk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was not written for the contest. We started this back in mid January before the contest was even announced.

I also disagree that there's any real competition or strife in the article. No one saw each other's parts fully, so no one was outright trying to one-up someone else. Each author was just going along with whatever line was given to them. I particularly found it interesting how more than one author was creating narratives that would allow for this discongruence that we all expected would happen.

I also disagree that it makes no sense. There is a degree of sense in here that I can personally see. But since it was written with no overall narrative, it only makes sense that there isn't really one there. However, I think there's plenty in the article to allow the reader to easily come up with one of their own.

In the end, it becomes something more complete than the sum of its parts, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is, quite clearly, not an Exquisite Corpse Contest entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The imagery is great and all but the narrative's all over the place. Given that this is part of the Exquisite Corpse contest, I wouldn't doubt if the sheer amount of authors that worked on this was what led to the mish-mash of ideas.

The internal conflict between the authors shows through the surface of the article's skin. I don't mind if it makes no sense, just as long as it has some sort of internal logic that can tie it thematically.

But since this is part of the contest, all of that strife between the authors trying to put their ideas into the article itself caused it to fall apart and get crushed under its own weight of ideas and concepts.

All in all, this is gets an uneasy downvote from me.

item.title: I'm not commander shephard but I'd thought I'd throw my comments into the ring too.
item.content:encoded:
What I particularly enjoy about this SCP is that it puts into perspective what this wiki is really about: art. Although the piece may not be easy to comprehend, it definitely makes a statement about what can be considered an SCP and how disjointed its parts can be correlated while still being cohesive. Surrealist pieces have been done before (a la SCP-3999), but this, I think, really encapsulates that genre.

Do I think the ultimate product outside of it being an exquisite corpse is amazing? Not particularly. But this is definitely a fun experiment to watch unfold and its atmosphere combined with its surrealist and bizarre nature incline me to upvote. I don't think we need any more SCPs like this, though — one is just fine.

I also have my own short theory about what's going on, though it's technically not the correct theory, since there is no correct theory:

Copy + Pasting from Discord
- Hide Spoilers

Some eldritch, human-like entity with infectious properties arrived from another dimension/time with the purpose of reconstructing the present to make a good future for humanity, but ultimately couldn't find a single one, and thus self-destructed.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
I think he meant the latter? I'm not entirely sure. He is technically correct in that Personnel itself is said in the singular but is referring to a collective.

Either way, it didn't work in the context of the sentence so I changed it to "individual."

item.title: Hi, I am Commander Sheppard and this is my favorite comment on the citadel.
item.content:encoded:
I love this comment. You managed to put into words the particular reason why I love this article so much.

We really did not have any intention to post it with little editing initially. It wasn't until after we finished, collectively looked at it, and saw just how… bizarrely coherent it was that we decided to just run with it.

Yes, it has its issues and as a more "traditional" SCP, I'd consider it largely flawed in its execution. But looking at it from the more meta perspective of how it was constructed, seeing these bizarre threads of ideas weave themselves in and out through each person's addition, finding some cohesiveness in this thing that shouldn't really have any cohesiveness… I can't help but love it.

I believe we plan on reworking the final product at some point to make something more traditional in its construction. However, this one is going to stay on its own (unless it gets deleted or something. Hopefully not.)

There's a beauty in an Exquisite Corpse that I think makes this worthwhile. Even the name, Exquisite Corpse came from the original Surrealist artists first time playing the game: "The exquisite corpse shall drink the new wine."

I think this quote from André Breton goes well enough into why I think a true Exquisite Corpse is so special:

Ill-disposed critics in 1925-1930 gave further example of their ignorance when they reproached us for delighting in such childish distractions, and at the same time suspected us of having produced such monsters in broad daylight, individually, and more or less laboriously. In fact, what excited us about these productions was the assurance that, for better or worse, they bore the mark of something which could not be created by one brain alone, and that they were endowed with a much greater leeway, which cannot be too highly valued by poetry.
Finally, with the Exquisite Corpse we had at our command an infallible way of holding the critical intellect in abeyance, and of fully liberating the mind's metaphorical activity.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
"personnel" is actually both a singular and a plural noun

I've always only ever seen it used as plural (since it refers to people, which is plural). Is there a reference for the singular definition where "personnel" would refer to one person?

Or maybe I'm misreading this statement and "singular" is meant in the sense that we don't say "personnels" for multiple groups of personnel, since personnel refers to a collective.

English y u be lik dis

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I was initially disappointed by the realization that there is no *conscious* attempt at any consistent internal logic in this piece, I'm realizing this works better when judged as a setting rather than as a story; it's dreamlike and self-contradicting not just because it's designed as such by a singular author, but rather because it forms a really fascinating picture of what a part of the SCP universe could look like in the collective consciousness of a group of talented authors. If you think it's being written by a single author, it feels like a blurry and overly-ambitious attempt at obfuscation, too obtuse to have value. Knowing the writing process that this emerged from makes it much more interesting and artistically valuable. +1

+ my interpretation
- very cool

The reader trying to find the plotline of the piece is very much like the Foundation classifying the location as an anomaly at all (be that Keter or Thaumiel or whatever else); they're trying to understand and find an internal logic to something that truly has no guiding principles. At the end, when the Foundation reclassifies it as Neutralized, that's akin to the realization that the location is directly situated in humanity/the Foundation/the writers' collective consciousness—hence, it's an "expected consequence of human sapient extrapolation."

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: No problem! I think I'm gonna stick with my downvote for now, but if you ever make any major changes or want another look at it feel free to contact me.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
Gotcha, I fixed the ones you pointed out plus a few others. If you find any more, I would be happy to nab them as well!

I am notoriously bad at recognizing poor clinical tone, so I appreciate the assist!

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Noting that "personnel" is actually both a singular and a plural noun.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
resulting in the anomalous property that both SCP-5633 and Site Mu-18 are temporally shifting to another reality.

Though when examined all blood in the vial was incinerated and the DNA could not be identified.

A D-Class personnel

These are the ones that stuck out to me on my first reading. The first one I listed isn't exactly erroneous, but it's definitely awkward to read. Same with the second, and the third is that "personnel" is plural.

I know I saw some more, so if choobakka doesn't mention them in their comment I'll come back and list what else I find.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
clinical tone errors

Feel free to point these out and I will see about fixing them.
When it isn't fully diving into fantastical incoherency (which was really good!), there are just a lot of strange and jerky clinical tone errors and mistakes that took me out of the piece.

item.title: BOO! This was actually written as an Exquisite Corpse.
item.content:encoded:
So, all of y'all who are saying it is incoherent or difficult to understand…. there's largely a reason for that. This was actually NOT written by only me. In fact, I only wrote the Containment Procedures. From there, it was written as an Exquisite Corpse by all the authors mentioned in the original credits.

We wanted to initially post it to see how the reaction would be without this knowledge and it was… not too far from what we expected :p

It was fun to see, though!

If you guys have any theories as to an internal story or coherent logic to the whole thing, stick with it! Maybe there is! I hope the knowledge of this doesn't ruin the article for anyone.

Thanks guys!

PS. We started writing this back in mid January so the Exquisite Corpse contest totally stole our secret idea from us! /s

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna novote this SCP for now, mainly due to me not really understanding what's happening for the most part (or at least the Experiment Log 5633-█-█ part of the SCP).

+ My critique and issues
- My critique and issues
  • The formatting of the dates seem to switch around between Year/Month/Day, Month/Day/Year, and Day/Month/Year. I'd suggest to be consistent and only use 1 date format.
  • I think I understand that something changed history, but it started to get confusing as it went on. I can understand that there's time travel-wackery going on though.
  • The Experiment Log 5633-█-█ part specifically confuses me the most. To me, it seem to slowly not make sense.
  • You begin to introduce new instances of SCP-5633, but never really explain what they are. Like who or what are SCP-5633-β, SCP-5633-δ, SCP-5633-γ, and SCP-5633-Γ? When I was writing this, I thought that they could be Foundation members, but I don't think I got that until after.
  • I do like the ACS header, but it changes fairly quickly. I had to wait for it to go through a few times before I knew what each version said. I'd suggest slowing the switch speed of the header (assuming you can)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh hey, I hadn't seen that article. That's neat!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending is far too incoherent to have any impact. If there's actually some internal logic, it's too obtuse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the Object Class bit, I think that's amazing. And there are other good ideas buried in here, but… Unfortunately the article is too all over the place for me. Sections are disjointed from each other, which is a choice, but even more frustrating is that the internal consistency of each section is also screwy and it doesn't seem like an intentional choice in the same way. The second addendum strikes me as particularly egregious in this regard, and I'm not the biggest fan of simply… lampshading nonstandard entries.

I can tell there's a lot of talent that went into this! But my takeaway here is that it was difficult and frustrating to read, and not in a way that felt rewarding or meaningful.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The experiment log where the corpse demands blood is excellent; unfortunately, most of the surrounding sections feel too disjointed, both in relation to each other and even within themselves, to engage me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is the second article this month that's done the changing-header gimmick. the other one used it better. the wording in your description is incredibly awkward in a number of places, to the extent that i stopped reading because i got annoyed. downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The part of this that I don’t like is the ending. Unless I missed something, it feels more incoherent than the rest of the article does, and it’s a damper on what is otherwise a very good execution of a hard-to-execute idea.

I still enjoyed it, though. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On XXXX/XX/XX it was noted

Missed something here

Also, this looks interesting! I really like the images.

item.title: SCP-5633
item.content:encoded:
This Will Require A Great Amount of Blood

So, this was written as an Exquisite Corpse that we started on Jan. 23rd and ended about a week ago.

It started with the initial photo and the title and went from there! Each member was given a single line from the person who wrote prior to them and allowed to go nuts from there.

These are all the authors that contributed:

  • Captain Kirby
  • JackalRelated
  • aismallard
  • Modulum
  • Uncle Nicolini
  • Sterbai
  • Yossipossi
  • Alces_alces
  • magna2s
  • Tufto
  • Rounderhouse

Image Sources:

First image is taken by myself, Woedenaz and released under CC BY-SA 3.0.
Second image is taken from here and released under CC0 1.0.
Third image is a still taken from here and released under CC0 1.0 according to Florida Law.
